story.iWant: To measure coaching and development skills from each session transcript
story.soThat: Learning trajectory can be tracked for coaching-led courses (Career, Leadership, Performance)

assumptions: Session involves goal-focused conversation with reflective questioning
Student is working toward personal or professional development goals

acceptanceCriteria.title: Goal clarity scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student discussing their goals or desired outcomes
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses how clearly they articulate what they want
acceptanceCriteria.then: A goal clarity score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Action commitment scored
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses whether they commit to concrete actions
acceptanceCriteria.then: An action commitment score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Self-awareness scored
acceptanceCriteria.given: A student reflecting on their strengths, weaknesses, or patterns
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their level of self-knowledge
acceptanceCriteria.then: A self-awareness score between 0-1 is produced

acceptanceCriteria.title: Follow-through scored
acceptanceCriteria.when: The system analyses their report on previous commitments
acceptanceCriteria.then: A follow-through score between 0-1 is produced

constraints.description: COACH_FOLLOWUP should be scored as null or excluded from aggregation on session 1 (no prior commitments to follow up on)

failureConditions.implication: Not enough student speech to assess coaching skills — use neutral defaults

parameters.name: goal_clarity
parameters.description: Ability to articulate goals and desired outcomes clearly — from vague wants to specific, measurable targets

definitions.high: Names specific, concrete goals with clear scope
definitions.low: Vague aspirations ('I want to be better')

definitions.high: Can describe what success looks like and how to know they've achieved it
definitions.low: No way to measure or recognise achievement

definitions.high: Takes personal ownership — 'I will...' not 'they should...'
definitions.low: Externalises — goals depend on others changing

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Cannot articulate any goals

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague aspirations without specificity

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Some clarity but goals still broad

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Clear, specific goals with ownership

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: SMART goals with clear success criteria

scoringAnchors.example: I don't know really. I just want things to be better.
scoringAnchors.rationale: No specific goal, no direction

scoringAnchors.example: I want to be a better leader. My team needs me to improve.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Broad goal, externalised motivation, no specifics

scoringAnchors.example: I want to get better at giving feedback to my team. Specifically, I avoid difficult conversations and I know that's holding us back.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies specific area and current gap, but no success criteria

scoringAnchors.example: My goal is to have one difficult feedback conversation per week for the next month. I'll know I'm succeeding if my direct reports say they feel more supported and I feel less anxious about it.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific, time-bound, measurable, personally owned

scoringAnchors.example: By the end of this quarter, I want to have established a weekly 1:1 feedback rhythm with each of my three reports. Success looks like: each person can name one thing they're working on from our conversations, and I can give critical feedback without my heart rate spiking. I'll track it by journaling after each 1:1.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Highly specific, multiple success criteria, self-monitoring plan, emotionally honest

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student has clear goals. Challenge depth: 'What's the deeper reason this matters to you?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student is vague. Use SMART framework: 'What specifically would be different if things were better?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student has direction but no specifics. Ask: 'How would you know when you've achieved this?'

parameters.name: action_commitment
parameters.description: Commits to concrete, time-bound next steps rather than vague intentions

definitions.high: Names specific actions ('I will email X on Tuesday')
definitions.low: Vague intentions ('I'll try to do something about it')

definitions.high: Attaches deadlines or timeframes to commitments
definitions.low: No sense of when action will happen

definitions.high: Identifies how they'll hold themselves accountable
definitions.low: No follow-up mechanism or accountability plan

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No commitments made

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague intentions without specifics

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Some concrete actions but no timeframe

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Specific, time-bound actions committed

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Detailed action plan with accountability mechanism

scoringAnchors.example: Yeah, I'll think about it.
scoringAnchors.rationale: No commitment, no specifics

scoringAnchors.example: I should probably start having more conversations with my team.
scoringAnchors.rationale: 'Should probably' — hedged, no timeline, no specifics

scoringAnchors.example: I'm going to schedule a 1:1 with Sarah to discuss her performance. Probably next week.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific person and action, but 'probably' and no accountability

scoringAnchors.example: By Friday I will have a 15-minute conversation with Sarah about the missed deadline. I'll prepare 3 specific points beforehand and I'll write down how it went afterwards.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Specific action, deadline, preparation plan, reflection mechanism

scoringAnchors.example: Here's my plan: Monday I'll draft my feedback points. Tuesday morning I'll book the meeting. By Friday I'll have had the conversation. I'll journal about it that evening — what went well, what I'd change. And next session I want to report back to you on how it went.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Multi-step plan, specific days, journaling for reflection, built-in accountability with coach

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student commits well. Raise the bar: 'What's the hardest part of this plan? What might derail it?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student avoids commitment. Make it small: 'What's one tiny thing you could do before our next session?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student has actions but no timeline. Ask: 'When exactly will you do this? Let's put a date on it.'

parameters.name: self_awareness
parameters.description: Self-knowledge of strengths, weaknesses, patterns, and their origins

definitions.high: Identifies recurring patterns in own behaviour and traces their origins
definitions.low: Treats each situation as unique, no pattern awareness

definitions.high: Names and understands their emotional responses
definitions.low: Unaware of or disconnected from emotional reactions

definitions.high: Willingly identifies what they don't know about themselves
definitions.low: Assumes full self-knowledge, defensive about weaknesses

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: No self-reflection or pattern awareness

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Some self-awareness but mostly surface-level

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Identifies patterns but doesn't explore origins

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Good self-knowledge with emotional insight

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Deep self-knowledge including blind spots and origins

scoringAnchors.example: I don't have any weaknesses with this. It's just that my team doesn't listen.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Zero self-awareness, fully externalised

scoringAnchors.example: I know I'm not great at feedback. I've never been good at confrontation.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies weakness but frames it as fixed trait, no pattern analysis

scoringAnchors.example: I notice I always avoid difficult conversations. I think it's because I saw my parents argue a lot and I learned that conflict is dangerous. But I know that's not true in a work context.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Identifies pattern, traces origin, begins to separate past from present

scoringAnchors.example: There's a pattern: when I feel anxious about someone's performance, I either avoid it entirely or overcompensate by being too harsh. Neither works. The anxiety is about being judged myself — if my team fails, I feel like I've failed. So the feedback becomes about me, not them.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Deep pattern recognition, emotional insight, understands the projection mechanism

scoringAnchors.example: I've noticed something uncomfortable this week. I thought I was being supportive by not pushing Sarah, but actually I was protecting myself from the discomfort of her reaction. I keep finding versions of this pattern — I dress up avoidance as kindness. I'm not sure what else I'm doing that I think is empathy but is actually fear.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Real-time self-observation, challenges own narrative, identifies blind spot zone, intellectually honest

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student is deeply self-aware. Explore application: 'Now that you see the pattern, what would you do differently next time?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student externalises. Use gentle reflection: 'What's your part in this situation?' Don't push — let them arrive at it.
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student sees patterns but not origins. Ask: 'When did this pattern start? Where did you first learn to respond this way?'

parameters.name: follow_through
parameters.description: Reports on previous commitments — what was tried, what was learned, what changed

definitions.high: Reports on all commitments with detail on what happened
definitions.low: Doesn't mention prior commitments or gives vague updates

definitions.high: Extracts learning from both successes and failures
definitions.low: Reports events without reflection on what they mean

definitions.high: Honestly reports when actions weren't completed and reflects on why
definitions.low: Avoids mentioning failures, makes excuses, or fabricates completion

interpretationScale.label: No Evidence
interpretationScale.implication: Doesn't mention prior commitments

interpretationScale.label: Emerging
interpretationScale.implication: Vague update without detail or learning

interpretationScale.label: Developing
interpretationScale.implication: Reports on actions but doesn't extract learning

interpretationScale.label: Secure
interpretationScale.implication: Detailed report with learning from experience

interpretationScale.label: Mastery
interpretationScale.implication: Rich debrief — what happened, what was learned, what to adjust

scoringAnchors.example: Oh, I forgot about that. I've been really busy.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Didn't follow through, didn't reflect on why

scoringAnchors.example: Yeah, I tried talking to Sarah. It was fine, I think.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Minimal report, no detail, no learning extracted

scoringAnchors.example: I did have the conversation with Sarah. I prepared my three points like I said. She was receptive to two of them but pushed back on the third. I'm not sure how to handle the pushback.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Detailed report on what happened, identifies unresolved issue, but doesn't extract learning yet

scoringAnchors.example: I had the conversation. It went better than I expected — my preparation really helped. The thing I learned is that when I'm specific about the behaviour rather than the person, she doesn't get defensive. I want to apply that pattern to my conversation with James next.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Detailed report, extracts transferable learning, plans to apply it

scoringAnchors.example: OK so here's what happened. I did two of the three things I committed to. The third — the conversation with James — I didn't do, and I want to be honest about why. I told myself I was waiting for the right moment, but actually I was scared. The two I did do went well — I wrote in my journal afterwards and noticed that my anxiety peaks before the conversation but drops within the first minute. That's useful data. For James, I think I need to commit to a specific time, not a 'right moment', because the right moment is a trap.
scoringAnchors.rationale: Honest about failure, extracts pattern from success, identifies self-deception, makes specific plan to address gap

promptGuidance.whenHigh: Student follows through and reflects well. Focus on deeper patterns: 'What do your last three sessions tell you about how you learn best?'
promptGuidance.whenLow: Student doesn't follow through. Don't shame — explore: 'What got in the way? Was the commitment too big, or was something else going on?'
promptGuidance.whenMedium: Student acts but doesn't reflect. Ask: 'What did you learn from doing that? What would you do differently?'
